intro: 13 Scales places you on thirteen political axes as well as three compasses: economic and class, social, and foreign policy. It also gives you an overall ideology and stance. You answer 50 statements, using a slider to show how strongly you agree or disagree with each one.

stances.name: Reactionary
stances.description: Extremely right-wing. Wants to reverse major social, economic, or cultural changes and restore an earlier order.

stances.name: Conservative
stances.description: Strongly right-wing. Defends tradition and opposes further change.

stances.name: Center-Right
stances.description: Moderately right-wing. Leans on tradition but accepts gradual reform.

stances.name: Center-Left
stances.description: Moderately left-wing. Wants reform but not a fundamental break from established institutions and traditions.

stances.name: Liberal
stances.description: Strongly left-wing. Wants broad reforms to change society.

stances.name: Progressive
stances.description: Extremely left-wing. Wants fundamental reform of the current order.

ideologies.name: Traditionalist Conservatism
ideologies.description: Backs a strong government that enforces economic and social conservatism and pursues national interests abroad.

ideologies.name: Paleoconservatism
ideologies.description: Backs a strong government that enforces economic and social conservatism, but keeps foreign intervention limited.

ideologies.name: Neoconservatism
ideologies.description: Wants a limited federal government at home while staying economically and socially conservative. Backs strong foreign intervention to advance national interests.

ideologies.name: Right-Libertarianism
ideologies.description: Wants a limited federal government at home while staying economically and socially conservative. Keeps foreign intervention limited.

ideologies.name: Social Democracy
ideologies.description: Wants a strong government to reduce social and economic inequality. Backs intervention abroad to promote human rights.

ideologies.name: Modern Liberalism
ideologies.description: Wants a strong government to reduce social and economic inequality. Backs intervention abroad to advance national interests.

ideologies.name: Left-Libertarianism
ideologies.description: Wants a limited government but supports social and economic change that reduces inequality. Favors little foreign intervention, focused on human rights.

ideologies.name: National Liberalism
ideologies.description: Accepts economic hierarchy but supports social liberties and the rights of marginalized racial, gender, and sexual minorities. Uses government to protect those rights and keeps foreign intervention limited.

ideologies.name: Neoliberalism
ideologies.description: Accepts economic hierarchy but supports social liberties and the rights of marginalized racial, gender, and sexual minorities. Uses government to protect those rights and embraces foreign intervention.

ideologies.name: Neolibertarianism
ideologies.description: Accepts economic hierarchy but supports social liberties and the rights of marginalized racial, gender, and sexual minorities. Wants limited government at home and an active one abroad.

ideologies.name: Neoclassical Liberalism
ideologies.description: Accepts economic hierarchy but supports social liberties and the rights of marginalized racial, gender, and sexual minorities. Wants limited government at home and abroad.

ideologies.name: Paternalistic Conservatism
ideologies.description: Socially conservative, but uses a strong central government to elevate the economically struggling.

ideologies.name: Conservative Populism
ideologies.description: Socially conservative and hostile to economic elites. Skeptical of strong government.

scales.name: Populism vs Establishmentarianism
poles: Populism treats educational status as less important.
Establishmentarianism treats it as more important.

scales.name: Equality vs Hierarchy
poles: Equality sees economic divisions as harmful.
Hierarchy sees them as inevitable.

scales.name: Protection vs Free Trade
poles: Protection sees foreign trade as harmful to national interests.
Free Trade sees it as improving competitiveness.

scales.name: Interventionism vs Laissez-Faire
poles: Interventionism favors government involvement in the economy.
Laissez-Faire opposes it.

scales.name: Racial Equality vs Status Quo
poles: Racial Equality sees the current system as bad for historically marginalized racial groups.
Status Quo sees it as fair.

scales.name: Feminism vs Traditionalism
poles: Feminism sees traditional gender norms as oppressive.
Traditionalism sees them as positive.

scales.name: Open Borders vs Closed Borders
poles: Open Borders sees immigration as an asset.
Closed Borders sees it as a liability.

scales.name: Liberty vs Morality
poles: Liberty treats so-called degenerate behavior as a personal right.
Morality treats it as harmful and needing correction.

scales.name: Pro-LGBTQIA+ vs Anti-LGBTQIA+
poles: Pro-LGBTQIA+ supports the community and pushes for greater acceptance.
Anti-LGBTQIA+ opposes it.

scales.name: Critical vs Loyal
poles: Critical sees the nation as fundamentally flawed and wants schools to teach those flaws.
Loyal sees it as fundamentally great and wants schools to teach that greatness.

scales.name: Centralism vs Confederalism
poles: Centralism trusts the federal government to govern best.
Confederalism trusts state governments.

scales.name: Humanitarianism vs Productivism
poles: Humanitarianism says government should promote human rights abroad.
Productivism says it should promote economic interests.

scales.name: Militarism vs Pacifism
poles: Militarism wants a strong military and the will to use it.
Pacifism wants to cut military intervention down.
